As well as for special forces of the Ministry of Internal Affairs and the army. "Chestnut" is built on the basis of automatic with a free shutter. The fire is fired from the open bolt, the fire mode switch (which is also a fuse) is located on the left above the trigger guard. The barrel box is stamped out of steel, the pistol grip with the trigger guard and the fore-end are cast of plastic. The trunk of "Chestnut" has a polygonal cut and can be equipped with a removable silencer. The standard sighting devices consist of a fly and a fly-away head for two distances - 50 and 100 meters, in addition, the latest versions of "Chestnut" can be equipped with detachable collimating sights ("red dot"). The AEK-919K retractable attachment, made of metal, can be rotated 180 degrees downward for a more comfortable attachment when pulled out. In general, "Chestnut" is described as an accurate and reliable weapon, which has small dimensions. In my opinion, the main disadvantage of PP AEK-919K is the location of the translator / fuse - to remove the weapon from the fuse with the right hand, without rotating the weapon in the palm of your hand, it is almost impossible even for a person with long fingers. If it is impossible to use the second hand, this drawback may cost the shooter extra seconds of time, and maybe life. The collimator sight, advertised together with "Kashtan" at present, is quite large, and is intended rather for use in police operations, since it considerably facilitates the aiming at the expense of increasing inconvenience when carrying a weapon. However, it is possible to install more modern and compact sights.

FN P90 - Belgian submachine gun (personal self-defense weapon), developed in 1986-1987 by FN Herstal. It was developed, first of all, for tankmen and drivers of military vehicles. Has a progressive and convenient ergonomic design. Specially for the P90, a 5.7 ? 28 mm SS190 cartridge was developed, which has a high breakdown power and a low ricochet. The bullet of this cartridge develops a muzzle velocity up to 715 m / s and is capable of penetrating a titanium / Kevlar bulletproof vest that meets the requirements of NATO CRISAT from 200 meters. The magazine is installed on top of the receiver and its capacity is 50 cartridges, while the cartridges are horizontally perpendicular to the trunk and unfolded 90 degrees before being fed into the chamber. It is made of transparent plastic, which allows the shooter at any time to know the number of cartridges in it. The store is filled in the usual way. The shell cases are thrown down, through the hollow handle of the fire control. FN P90 is equipped with a built-in collimating sight with a multiplicity of 1X with an aiming grid in the form of the letter T. The aiming grid has an automatically adjustable backlight depending on the ambient light; Also, the weapon is equipped with a laser target designator, located at the bottom. In addition, a silencer is attached to it, but its operation is possible only if cartridges with subsonic speed are used. At the same time, one of the main tasks was the maximum reduction in the weight of weapons and ammunition. The Belgian designers followed the path of creating a small-caliber cartridge with a high initial velocity of the bullet and developed a munition corresponding to the requirements that had been set up, which also had a flat trajectory of the bullet's flight and yielded a small amount of return, which facilitated fire and defeat of the enemy for inexperienced soldiers with weak fire training. Such a weapon-cartridge complex was intended in the future to replace pistols and submachine guns in the operating armies of NATO countries with a traditional 9 mm Parabellum cartridge.

The Heckler - Koch HK MP7 submachine gun, developed in the late 1990s by the German firm Hekler and Koch, belongs to the new class of small arms, the Personal Defense Weapon (PDW), in the western classification, and is intended for the arming of servicemen who The state does not have a full-fledged assault rifle (assault rifle), that is, crews of military equipment, gun calculations, etc. From a technical standpoint, the HK MP7A1-automatic weapons under specially designed cartridge calibre 4.6 mm (cartridge designation 4.6 h30mm, Chuck recalls reduced cartridge 5.56 mm NATO). HK MP7A1 is organized by type of compact pistol-machine gun shop is inserted into the grip, collapsible stock, telescopic front has an extra foldable handle for two-handed weapons retention. Automation HK MP7A1 built on a scheme with a gas engine with a short stroke of the gas piston, locking - turning the bolt. In general, the automation and USMHK MP7A1 strongly resemble the corresponding elements of the G36 rifle of the same company. The case of HKMP7A1 is made of plastic, on the top of the case guides are provided for fixing various sighting devices. Fire modes - single shots and and turns, the fire mode switch also serves as a fuse and is controlled from both sides of the weapon. The cocking handle is located on the rear side of the body above the butt, has a T-shape (like the handle of the M16 rifle) and is stationary when firing. The fire is conducted from a closed shutter.

When creating this submachine gun, the goal was to maximize the accuracy of shooting with automatic fire, while maintaining small dimensions and weight of weapons and using sufficiently powerful ammunition.45 ACP, which are distinguished by high stopping action at short distances. The Kriss Super V submachine gun uses a rather unusual automatic scheme with a semi-free shutter and redirection of the momentum of the moving parts moving down. The lightweight shutter of the submachine gun has side protrusions that enter the inclined grooves of the massive rocker moving inside the hull of the gun up and down. When a shot is fired, the movement of the shutter is converted back into a vertical movement of the balance beam downward, while at the expense of the variable angle of the groove along which the shutter protrusions slide, an early retraction of the shutter slows it. The shooting is carried out with a closed shutter, the trigger trigger is located above the axis of the barrel. The cocking cocking handle is foldable, is located on the left on the body of the weapon and remains stationary while shooting. The fire mode interpreter is located on the trigger box approximately in the middle of the weapon's length, and provides fire control by single shots and bursts. A separate fuse is located above the pistol grip. To enhance the sustainability of weapons and further reduce podbrosa barrel while shooting without using a pistol grip stock is raised relative to the axis of the barrel so that the recoil shoulder towards the retention arms hand minimum (such a decision before it was used in the Soviet Olympic-calibre pistol MC-3, also known as "Sheptarskogo frame"). The store receiver is located in front of the fire control handle. Currently, 13-charging magazines are used from Glock 21 pistols, but in the future it is planned to develop own stores of greater capacity. To increase the accuracy of the shooting, the Kriss Super V submachine gun is equipped with a foldable side butt and a removable front handle.

Sub-machine gun PP-19 Bison was designed in the early 1990's. At the Izhevsk Machine-Building Plant with the aim of arming the MVD with modern small-sized automatic small arms instead of AKS-74U. In this case, the PP-19 itself was designed and manufactured with a large volume of Kalashnikov AK-74 assault rifles. Such details include a shortened AK-74 receiver with a pistol grip for controlling fire and a trigger mechanism, as well as a metal folding butt, borrowed from the AK-74 automatic. Submachine gun PP-19 Bison differs from its original screw magazine with a capacity of 64 cartridges for PP-19 Bizon-2 for the cartridge 9x18 PM and 53 cartridges for PP-19 Bizon-2-01 for the cartridge 9x19 Parabellum. This store was created in part based on the American Calico pistol. At the same shop PP-19 is placed on the gun with great convenience for the shooter and combines the function of the forehead. The store is located under the trunk, does not increase the size of the submachine gun and performs the function of foremost to retain weapons when firing. For convenience of carrying and transportation, the submachine gun has a folding butt. Two variants of the stock are possible: a frame stock, folded to the left side or an upper butt that folds onto the receiver's lid. The design of the submachine gun allows you to fire with a folded butt. Bison is perfectly adaptable for use in conjunction with silent shooting devices and modern fire control equipment. There is a side bar for installation of collimating, optical and night sights.

The PP-2000 submachine gun was developed at the Design Instrument Design Bureau in the city of Tula, Russia, and was first shown in public in 2004, although a patent for its design was registered back in 2001. PP-2000 is obviously intended for the role of either self-defense weapons of the military (PDW), or as a melee weapon for special operations forces, both army and police / police, primarily for action in the city. PP-2000 is extremely compact and light, with a minimum number of parts and a simple design, which ensure simple operation and low cost. The ability to use 7N21 and 7N31 armor-piercing ammunition, originally developed for the GSh-18 pistol, makes it possible to use the PP-2000 to fight opponents in individual means of protection (helmets, body armor) and effectively hit targets inside the vehicles. In comparison with the small-caliber analogues produced in Western countries, such as Belgian 5.7mm FN P90 or German 4.6mm HK MP-7, the PP-2000 thanks to the use of 9mm bullets provides greater efficiency against targets not protected by bulletproof vests. At present, the PP-2000 is already in series production and is being used by the Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs. The submachine gun PP-2000 is built on the basis of automation with a free shutter. Fire from the PP-2000 is carried out with a closed shutter, trigger trigger mechanism trigger. The body of the submachine gun is made of plastic with a pistol grip and an enlarged trigger guard, providing, if necessary, holding the weapon with both hands. The bolt protrudes from the body above the barrel, in its front part there is a leaning arm to the right or to the left. The magazine is inserted into the pistol grip, the store latch button is located at the base of the trigger guard. The fuse interpreter is located on the left side of the gun, above the pistol grip, and provides firing both single shots and bursts. A distinctive feature of the PP-2000, protected by a patent - in the back of its housing is a nest for a spare store. When inserted in this jack store, it can be used as a rudimentary shoulder stop.

Submachine gun PP-19-01 "Vityaz" is a further development of the submachine gun PP-19 "Bison". "Vityaz" was developed by the IZHMASH concern specially for the demands of the detachment of the special forces of the Ministry of the Interior of Russia "Vityaz", from where it got its name. Currently, the submachine gun PP-19-01 "Vityaz" is in mass production and is already being supplied to the units of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Russia. Like its predecessor, the AK-74U automatic machine is based on the design of the PP-19-01, or the newer AK-104, the unification of the "Vityaz" with which the details are about 70%. The knight is produced in two versions - the basic PP-19-01 issp.10 and the modified PP-19-01 mod.20 "Vityaz-SN". The second option has improved ergonomics (due to the removal of the fuse and the handle of the shutter on the left side), as well as an additional guide such as Picatinny rail on the cover of the receiver, which allows the installation of various additional sighting devices. Submachine gun PP-19-01 "Vityaz" can use the majority of commercial and army versions of the cartridge 9x19, produced in the world, including domestic cartridges 9mm 7N21 with armor-piercing bullet. PP-19-01 can be equipped with silencer sound shot. Pistol-machine gun PP-19-01 "Vityaz" uses automatic with a free shutter. The fire is conducted from the closed shutter, the trigger and safety mechanisms along with the receiver are completely borrowed from the AK-74U automatic. From the bottom to the receiver there is a shaft for box-type double-row stores, designed specifically for PP-19-01. If necessary, stores can be stapled in pairs using a special staple supplied with the weapon, providing an accelerated recharge of weapons in battle. Frame, metal, folding sideways. On the left side of the barrel a standard for the domestic weapon is used for fixing the brackets for optical sights. The sight is sectoral, open, adjustable in range.

The Minimi machine gun was developed by the Belgian company FN Herstal in the mid-late 1970s and early 1980s and has been in serial production since about 1981. It is used by many countries, including Belgium itself, the USA (under the designation M249 SAW), Canada (designated C9), Australia (designated F-89) and many others. The machine gun is deservedly popular for its high mobility in combination with firepower, which considerably exceeds the firepower of such machine guns as RPK-74, L86A1 and others, built on the basis of automatic weapons, and not created from scratch as machine guns. A distinctive feature of the Minimi is the ability to use both metal tape (standard method) and NATO standard rifles (from the M16 rifle, the backup version) without any changes in the design (similar to the Czech manual VZ.52 machine gun created by the 30 Years earlier). Minimi machine guns are used to increase the firepower of infantry units, providing effective fire at ranges up to 600-800 meters, combined with high mobility. Minimi - light (manual) machine gun, built on the basis of gas venting automatics, locking the barrel by turning the bolt. Power - metal loose tape or box magazines (the magazine receiver is located on the left side of the weapon under the receiver for the tape, the store is inserted at an angle of about 45 degrees down from the horizontal). When using the tape, the receiver window for stores is covered with a dust cover, when the magazine is inserted (with the tape removed), the open curtain overlaps the feed path of the tape. When using a tape, part of the energy of the gas engine is used to drag the tape, so the rate of fire with the tape is lower than when the magazine is powered. The tape is usually fed from plastic boxes or canvas bags on a metal frame attached to a machine gun from below, with a capacity of 100 or 200 cartridges. The barrel of the machine gun is quick-change, equipped with a flame arrester and a folding handle for carrying. The trunks of three basic sizes are produced: the standard length is 465 mm, the "landing" length is 349 mm and the "special purpose" is 406 mm long. Folding bipod, located under the barrel on the gas pipe. Depending on the country of manufacture and modification, the Minimi can have attachments and foreheads of various designs, mountings for optical and night sights, etc. Fire control - with the help of a pistol grip with a trigger, the fire mode is only automatic.

The PKM machine guns are highly reliable and enjoy a well-deserved popularity among the troops, despite the slightly over-complicated two-stage system of supplying cartridges from the tape to the trunk. Kalashnikov's machine gun uses gas-operated automation with a gas piston under the barrel with a long working stroke. The barrel is quick-change, has a handle for carrying, also used to replace the hot trunk. The gas outlet is equipped with a manual gas regulator. The barrel is locked by turning the bolt. Feeding cartridges - from an unassembled metal tape with a closed link. Tapes are assembled from pieces of 50 links with the help of a cartridge. The nominal capacity of the tapes is 100 (in manual version) or 200 (in the machine tool) cartridges. The tape feed direction is from right to left, the feed and exit windows of the tape are equipped with dust covers, as well as the window for ejecting the cartridge cases. Feeding cartridges from the tape two-stage - first a special grip when pulling back the original frame pulls the cartridge out of the tape back, after which the cartridge goes down to the line of delivery and at the back of the bolt is sent to the barrel. Shooting is carried out with an open bolt, only with automatic fire. Standard controls in the infantry version include a pistol grip, trigger, manual guard and frame stock. In armored vehicles, it is possible to install a special seatplate with paired handles and a trigger key instead of a butt, in the tank - an electric remote trigger mechanism is used. In the infantry version, the machine gun is equipped with a foldable two-legged bipod, the machine tool is additionally equipped with a universal tripod with an adapter for anti-aircraft shooting. The standard sighting devices were open, infantry versions were also produced with a side bracket for the installation of night sights.

In the mid-1950s, the Soviet Army launched a program to develop a new complex of small arms designed to replace the AK Kalashnikov assault rifle, the SCS carbine and the RAP hand-gun. The structure of the complex was to include an automatic machine and a maximum unified with it a manual machine gun (support weapons of the department), both for the cartridge 7.62x39 M43. According to the results of the competition in 1961, a modified AK Kalashnikov assault rifle and a Kalashnikov-made manual caliber RPK, unified with it in design and stores, were put into service with the SA. The PKK remained the main weapon of support for the department until 1974, when it was replaced by its analog for the 5.45x39 cartridge - the RPK-74 hand-gun. \n The Kalashnikov Kalashnikov machine gun uses the same automation scheme and basic design solutions as the AKM Kalashnikov assault rifle, that is, the gas-operated automation with locking the barrel by turning the bolt. The barrel box is stamped from steel sheet, more durable than the AKM box for increasing the resource. The trunk is longer than the AKM, there is no possibility of replacing it in case of overheating. Shock-trigger mechanism is completely analogous to that of AKM, allows firing single shots and bursts, shooting is conducted from a closed shutter. Feeding cartridges - from detachable stores, compatible with AK / AKM. For the PKK, two types of high-capacity magazines were developed and put into service: a boxed (carob) magazine for 40 cartridges and a drum magazine for 75 cartridges. Early versions of box-shaped stores were made of steel, later - from plastic. The drum shops had a steel structure and were distinguished by the high cost and slowness of the cartridges. The PKK was equipped with a folding two-legged bipod installed under the barrel, butt of a special shape and a sight with the possibility of introducing lateral corrections. The RPCS variant, developed for the airborne troops, had a folding side stock. In addition, versions of RPKN and RSKSN were produced with a bar mounted on the receiver for fixing the night sights. \n At present, the RPKM-7M machine gun is manufactured for the cartridge 7.62x39, intended primarily for export. It should be noted that as a handgun, the RPK had significant shortcomings - a small capacity of the power system, the inability to conduct intense automatic fire due to the non-replaceable trunk and firing from the closed shutter. Its main advantage was a high degree of unification with a standard AKM automatic machine, and somewhat larger in comparison with the range and accuracy of shooting (due to a longer and slightly heavier barrel).

RPG-7V1, hand-held anti-tank grenade launcher. It is designed to combat tanks, self-propelled artillery systems. The grenade launcher is based on the scheme of recoilless starting device of reusable application and a shot with an over-caliber warhead. The grenade launcher has a broadening of the barrel in its middle part - a charging chamber - for more complete utilization of the propellant energy, and a bell in the breech part - to ensure the smoothness of the complex. In addition to the mechanical sight, the grenade launcher has an optical sight. The constant development of new rounds of grenades with grenades of various striking actions, the improvement of sighting devices significantly expands the capabilities of the grenade launcher, making it multi-purpose.

The RG-6 grenade launcher (GRAU index 6G30) was developed in a hurry in 1993 at the TsKIB SOO (TsentralnoyKB Sport and Hunting weapons, Tula) to arm the troops that operated against the separatists in Chechnya. The small-scale production of the RG-6 was started in 1994 at the Tula Arms Factory, and the grenade launcher almost immediately began to arrive in the troops and some units of the Ministry of Internal Affairs. Due to the speed of development of the grenade launchers of early issues, there were some problems, in particular, the unreliable operation of USM. The RG-6 grenade launcher was created under the undoubted influence of the Milkor MGL South African grenade launcher, but due to the fact that the RG-6 uses fundamentally different ammunition with the departing sleeve, there are a number of notable differences in its design. \n The RG-6 grenade launcher has a revolver circuit, the drum chambers are formed by six rifled muzzle-charge barrels from the GP-25 grenade launchers. Accordingly, the equipment of the drum is carried out from the front, after folding the front part of the grenade launcher with the false trunk sideways and upwards. In the chambers grenades are held by special spring clips. Rotation of the drum is carried out by a spring that is manually cocked during recharging. The rotation of the drum after each shot occurs at the release of the ratchet at the time of release of the trigger, which excludes the attempt of repeated injection of a misfiring grenade. In addition, in the event of a protracted shot, the pomegranate immediately "leaves" from the trunk, and the author knows at least one case when a protracted shot at the RG-6 led to the destruction of the weapon (fortunately, the shooter threw the grenade launcher aside in time and nobody was hurt ). The shock-trigger mechanism is self-cocking, mostly borrowed from the GP-25 grenade launcher. The grenade launcher is equipped with a false trunk, which has a smooth (not cut) channel and serves only for fixing the front handle and sight. Sights folding, step type. The telescopic telescopic application has a rubber cushioning pad.

RGD-5 (hand grenade, remote, GRAU index - 57-G-717) - Soviet offensive hand grenade, refers to the anti-personnel fragmentation grenades of offensive offensive type. This means that it is designed to defeat the enemy's personnel with shell fragments during its explosion. The goal of the pomegranate is achieved by throwing a hand. Remote action - means that the grenade will explode after a certain time (3.2-4.2 seconds) after it is released, regardless of other conditions. Offensive type - means that the grenade fragments have a small mass and fly at a range less than the possible range of the throw. \n Externally, the pomegranate has an oval body made of thin steel. The streamlined body is assembled from the top and bottom parts, each of which includes an outer shell and a liner. The hole for the fuse during storage is closed with a plastic stopper. The mass of a grenade with a fuse of 310 g. The charge of an explosive is TNT with a mass of 110 grams. Range of fragmentation is 30 meters. \n The fuse of the grenade is universal, suitable also for grenades RG-42 and F-1. The fuse brand: UZRG, UZRGM (from the second half of the 1950s), or UZRGM-2. All these fuses are interchangeable.

F-1 (GRAU-57-G-721 index) is a hand-held anti-personnel defense grenade. It is designed to defeat manpower in a defensive battle. Due to the considerable radius of the fragmentation, it is possible to throw it only because of cover, from an armored personnel carrier or from a tank. \n The names "F-1" and slang "limonka" came from the French fragmentation grenade F-1 model of 1915 with a mass of 572 grams and the English grenade of the Lemon system, which were supplied to Russia during the First World War. Another version of the origin of the slang name is its shape resembling a lemon. \n Initially, the F-1 grenades were equipped with the fuse of FV Koveshnikov. In 1941, Ye. M. Vitseny and AA Bednyakov developed a universal UZRG fuse, after the war it was finalized and serves to this day under the name UZRGM (universal firing of hand grenades modernized).

A modern American hand grenade, commonly referred to as a concussion grenade, is designed to inflict damage primarily on the strength of the explosion. It also affects the fragments, but they play a secondary role here. 
 The main task of this grenade is the production of destruction (damage to unarmored or lightly armored vehicles, small structures (dugouts, cracks, rooms, fuel tanks, containers with property) and disabling enemy soldiers in confined spaces (in cars, shelters, dugouts, Premises). 
 A significant explosive effect is achieved due to a large charge of explosive (TNT) in garnet - 227 grams with a total mass of garnet of 443 grams. The length of the grenade is 13.8 cm. The diameter is about 5 cm. According to the American standards, the radius of death of a person with a shock wave is 2 meters. Fragments of a grenade can fly up to 200 meters. The radius of safe removal of their soldiers is 235 meters. 
 Applicable fuses M206A1 or M206A2. Grenade of remote action, i.e. The explosion occurs 4-6 seconds after the release of the clamping lever. Before the throw, the soldier presses the lever to the body of the grenade, pulls out the safety ring and throws the grenade at the target. You can throw a grenade only because of shelter. 
 The maximum range of the average soldier's throw is 40 meters. The pomegranate is painted black. The marking is yellow on the side surface of the grenade. This grenade became very popular during the Vietnam War in the sixties, when with their help, American soldiers destroyed Vietnamese people who were hiding in underground passages. It was not possible to do this with conventional grenades. Grenade can have a certain effectiveness in street fighting, mountain conditions. In the usual field battle, its effectiveness is questionable.

The offensive grenade M33 was designed as a grenade, which would be convenient for an accurate throw. The grenade has a spherical body with an internal notch. As a charge M33 is equipped with composition "B". When a grenade explodes, about 400 fragments form. The M33 grenade used the M204 series fuse. Based on the M33 grenade, its modification was developed - a grenade M67, which differed from the M67 only used fuse. In the M67 grenade, the M213 ignitor is used, which differs from the standard one by the presence of two safety devices - a conventional check with a ring and a wire loop wrapped around the fuse and holding the lever. Thanks to the reliable protection, which allows to carry a grenade, having hooked the lever of the fuse behind the equipment belt, and also to small dimensions with high density of fragments in the affected area, the grenades M67 were popular with paratroopers, marines and special forces. \n A further modification was the M68 grenade similar to the M59, which was supplied with a M217 fuse, but using a wire loop wrapped around the fuse and holding the lever in order to increase safety. To learn the rules of use is an imitation grenade M69, repeating the shape and weight of the grenade M67. In South Korea, under the designation K400, a copy of the M67 grenade with a modified fuse design is produced.
